text.content:
Phase 9 — NPC Index

Phase 9 introduces two new named NPCs and uses several conditional ones depending on prior-phase outcomes. This journal covers all of them.

The NPCs

  1. Route Warden Savax — new for Phase 9. The local antagonist. CR 5.
  2. The Unclaimed Witness (Merrit Osk) — new for Phase 9. Optional captive in the Sealed Witness Cell. CR 0.
  3. Haskur Vandrell — returning NPC. Use only if alive or relevant. Cross-references Phase 1–8 entries.
  4. Kethren Ilvath — returning NPC. Use only if alive or relevant. Cross-references Phase 6–8 entries.
  5. V. Korre — returning NPC. Use only if alive or relevant. Cross-references Phase 6–8 entries.
  6. Silent Courier — new statblock for Phase 9. Generic but distinct from the Phase 7 Silent Scout.
  7. Route-Cleaner Lead — already in Phase 8 actors. Re-used for Phase 9.
  8. Tally Sentinel — new construct. Optional; only activates on forced gate use.

How to use this journal

Each NPC entry covers:

  • Motive — what they want.
  • Public behavior — how they present.
  • Tell — the small physical signal that reveals their state.
  • Leverage — what the party can use against them.
  • What they know — concrete information they hold.
  • What they do not know — explicit limits on their knowledge.
  • Useful lines — verbatim dialogue the GM can speak.
  • Statblock suggestion — D&D 2024 / SRD-compatible build.

The NPCs in this phase are mostly not trying to kill the party. They are trying to classify, delay, redirect, or extract them. The GM should approach every NPC scene as a procedural negotiation first, a combat second.

Route Warden Savax

The local antagonist of Phase 9. Warden of Witness Station Seven, or acting warden during an emergency state. Not the mastermind. Not a cackling villain. A career procedural administrator who believes unauthorised passage is contamination.

Quick read

  • Indeterminate age — could be early forties, could be late sixties. Difficult to tell. Skin that has not seen sun in long enough that surface ageing markers do not apply.
  • Pale or shadowed features. Eyes that avoid the direct light of a torch — when speaking to a torch-carrying party, looks past their shoulder.
  • Careful diction. Gloves — thin, dark leather, well-fitted. Always worn.
  • Silence around the feet. Walks without making sound, not from stealth, but from a soft-soled habit.
  • Discomfort at open flame. Will request, politely, that an open torch be lowered or extinguished when conversing.

GM note: Do not confirm Savax's species in player-facing text. The physical details above are deliberately ambiguous. Some readers will assume Savax is a long-acclimated human. Some will suspect otherwise. Do not resolve. Phase 9's player-facing language constraint applies: no naming of deep factions or species.

Motive

Keep the waystation operational. Process all transit cleanly. Avoid creating procedural irregularities that would draw attention from the deeper authority. His career is the procedure. He is not personally invested in the Blooming Rot conspiracy; he administers transits, including this one, without judgment of their purpose.

Public behavior

Formal. Cold. Exact. Treats the party as misfiled bodies that need to be sorted. Will speak before fighting. Will offer dialogue mid-combat. Will not attempt to humanise the encounter: he does not care about the party as people. He cares about them as paperwork.

Tell

When Savax is genuinely uncertain about a procedural decision, he removes one glove — not both, just one — and flexes the hand briefly. Then he replaces the glove. This is the only physical sign that he is operating outside his comfort zone.

Leverage

  • Threat of procedural irregularity. The party can convince Savax that not classifying them is the bigger procedural risk. If they file (verbally) a category claim, Savax has to respond to it. Refusing to classify them becomes harder than processing them.
  • Exposure of upstream channel. If the party demonstrates they have evidence that Korre's channel was compromised by surface-side actors not authorised by the route, Savax will pause — this is not his information and he was not told. He may bargain for what they know.
  • Threat to damage records. Savax values records above his own life. The credible threat to scrape the Witness Registry Wall or to destroy the Conversion Note will get his attention.
  • Offer of clean retreat. If the party offers to leave the waystation without further damage in exchange for safe passage at the Black Ledger Gate, Savax will negotiate. He prefers ending the encounter without bodies.

What he knows

  • Haskur (Andren) transfer status. Knows Haskur was recovered, processed, and moved on through the Black Ledger Gate to the recovery waystation. Knows Haskur is currently classified as “recoverable but unreliable.”
  • Kethren's escort authority. Knows Kethren outranks him at this station. Knows Kethren's authority extends two waystations further down. Knows nothing of Kethren's origin.
  • The next route branch. Knows all three branches (recovery, escort, supply) and where they go. Will not disclose without leverage.
  • V. Korre's channel closure. Knows the channel was closed on direct instruction from the channel beyond the Black Ledger Gate. Knows Korre is now operating without route authorisation.
  • The deeper-authority mark exists. Has seen it on incoming dispatches. Treats it with formality. Will not discuss it. Will end conversations that approach it.

What he does not know

  • “E.”'s identity in direct terms. He knows the mark; he does not know who or what owns it.
  • Surface political details. He has heard of Caelith only in passing; he does not know about the Yeomanry Council.
  • Full Blooming Rot purpose. He knows recoveries are happening; he does not know why the recovery infrastructure was built on top of damage that was deliberately tested.
  • Whether the deeper authority is one being, multiple beings, an institution, or something else.

Useful lines

“Names are for surfaces.”

“If you are not witnessed, you are cargo.”

“You have descended without category.”

“Correction is still available.”

“I am the warden here. I am not the authority. I have been instructed.”

“You are speaking surface words. The gate does not listen to surface words. Speak again.”

“You bring evidence with you. Evidence is a category. We have a category for that.”

“I cannot answer that question. Whoever can is not currently present at this station. They have not been present at this station in my service. I do not expect them.”

Statblock suggestion

Use Bandit Captain (CR 2) with adjustments toward Tier 2:

  • HP 75 (10d8 + 30). AC 16 (studded leather + route brace).
  • Multiattack: short-sword + dagger; or, in command mode, foregoes attack to issue a command (one ally within 30 ft. makes an additional attack).
  • Procedural Command. Once per turn, when a route-cleaner or Silent Courier within 30 ft. of Savax takes the Disengage or Dodge action, that ally may also move up to 10 ft. as a reaction.
  • Route Cant. Savax can issue spoken commands in route shorthand that route personnel hear but the party does not understand without successful translation (Insight DC 17 catches the gist of his orders).
  • Skills: Investigation +6, Insight +6, Persuasion +5, Deception +5, History +6, Arcana +4.
  • Saving throws: Wis +5, Int +5.
  • Equipment: route lock-pin (a thin iron key for emergency gate releases), Warden Instruction Strip, a small bone fingerstamp matching his warden authority mark.

Effective CR: 5. Suitable for a Tier 2 party (8–11) as the lead opponent in a layered encounter, not as a solo boss.

Merrit Osk — The Unclaimed Witness

An optional NPC found in the Sealed Witness Cell. Former surface courier, taken below as a witness, awaiting transfer that has been deferred. Traumatized, hungry, precise in strange ways.

Quick read

  • Human. Mid-thirties. From Greyhawk City's Free Quarter. A working clerk for a Kestrel and Reed branch in Hardby; took on courier work for extra pay.
  • Brown hair pulled back severely. Practical clothing — surface-cut, surface-fabric, worn for three weeks without change. The clothing smells of dry stone and lamp oil and the witness cell's wax.
  • Steady hands. Precise speech — the speech pattern of someone who reads aloud for a living. Will not stop describing what is in front of her.

Motive

  • Go home. Her mother is in the Free Quarter. She does not want to be transferred further.
  • Not be witnessed. She has gathered enough fragmentary route information in three weeks to understand that being formally witnessed and transferred means becoming, in her words, “permanent cargo.”
  • Be useful. A clerk's instinct: she will give the party concrete, organised information in exchange for any prospect of leaving the cell alive.

Public behavior

Quiet. Precise. Will respond to direct questions with direct answers. Will not volunteer broadly — she does not know what is relevant, and she has learned in three weeks of holding that volunteering information is dangerous.

If the party offers her hope, she does not weep. She nods. The nodding is itself unsettling — the gesture of someone who has been keeping hope alive on rations.

Tell

When Merrit is lying or omitting, she folds her hands in her lap. Otherwise her hands rest on the bench. Insight DC 13 catches it. She is bad at lying.

Leverage

  • Take her out. The party offering to leave the waystation with her gets her active cooperation immediately. She will help with the Black Ledger Gate (her presence as a substitute witness opens Method A; her testimony as an oath-witness can open Method E).
  • Promise to contact her mother. Even if the party can't take her physically, promising to notify her mother gets her cooperation.
  • Religious appeal. Merrit is a Saint Cuthbert worshipper (the panic prayer in the Sleeping Alcoves was by another person, but Merrit also follows Cuthbert in a quiet surface way). A cleric of Cuthbert in the party can establish trust quickly.

What she knows

  • People are processed here by marks. She has watched the route at work for three weeks through her cell's water slot. She has seen tokens placed, marks cut, conversions filed.
  • Kethren-like couriers outrank surface handlers. She watched a senior route-cleaner defer to Kethren on a transit. The cleaner did not argue.
  • Surface names are burned or converted. She knows this from overheard conversation and from the smell of burning paper.
  • “Recovery” means a planned retrieval after controlled failure. She has overheard a route clerk explaining this to a junior in the corridor outside her cell. She is the only NPC in the waystation who will explain this term to the party in surface words.
  • The next gate leads to a larger route, not a settlement. She has heard route personnel discuss it as if going down to a road, not to a place.
  • The interviewer's face. She remembers being interviewed by a man in grey-and-bone clothing. She cannot recall his face. This is genuine. She is not protecting him. Something erased her memory of his features.

What she does not know

  • “E.”'s identity. She has never heard the letter.
  • The final destination of any of the routes.
  • Deep faction names.
  • The full purpose of Blooming Rot, beyond “a planned retrieval after controlled failure.”
  • What the interviewer (Kethren or a courier like him) actually is.

Useful lines

“They asked my name above. Below they asked what I proved.”

“I think I am awaiting transfer. I do not want to be transferred.”

“My mother is in the Free Quarter. She lives at fourteen Cooper's Lane.”

“I do not remember the interviewer's face. I have tried.”

“The water comes through the slot. It is always the same temperature. They are not unkind. They are not anything.”

“I think I have proved nothing. I think that is why I am still here.”

“If you take me out, I will not slow you. If you cannot take me out, please tell my mother I am sorry.”

Use cases

  1. Phase 9 Black Ledger Gate — Witness Method. Merrit can be declared as the substitute witness for a forged transfer, opening Method A more cleanly. This requires her consent. She will give it if the party offers her safe passage out.
  2. Phase 9 emotional anchor. Merrit is the only ordinary surface human at the waystation. Her presence reminds the party what is at stake — people, not just paperwork. If the GM wants Phase 9 to land emotionally, Merrit is the lever.
  3. Phase 10 carry-forward. If the party brings Merrit out, she can testify on surface terms. She can be presented to Iren as a sworn statement; to Caelith as a witness; to the Yeomanry Council as evidence. She is one of the most powerful surface-side assets the party can leave Phase 9 with.
  4. Phase 10 emotional anchor. If Merrit dies in Phase 9 or is left behind, the loss should weigh on the party in Phase 10. The GM can refer back: the panic prayer scratched in the Sleeping Alcoves was by another person like Merrit. There are more of them.

Statblock suggestion

Use Commoner (CR 0) with adjustments:

  • HP 12. AC 10.
  • Skills: Investigation +3, Insight +2, Sleight of Hand +1.
  • Special: Clerk's Eye. Merrit can read any surface-script document the party finds at advantage. She can also read route shorthand at base proficiency (DC 13 instead of the usual DC 15), but slowly.
  • Equipment: nothing. The route took her belt, her boots, and her courier satchel when she was first held.

Haskur Vandrell — Conditional Use

Returning NPC. Phase 9 uses Haskur in one of three states. Choose based on prior-phase outcomes.

State A — Alive and with the party

Haskur is a prisoner, traveling companion, or unwilling guide. He has been visibly unsettled since the Phase 8 descent. By Phase 9, the unsettlement is constant.

Behavior

  • He recognises the waystation in fragments. He does not remember coming through here, but the smell, the light quality, the silence of the wardens — these are all things he has experienced.
  • He may freeze briefly when seeing his “Andren” entry on the Witness Registry Wall. He has never seen his own conversion before.
  • He fears being “reclaimed.” The route's classification of him as “recoverable but unreliable” means it has not, in his understanding, fully released him — the route would prefer he were brought back into its custody.

Useful lines

“I was collected, not saved.”

“They do not trust names down here.”

“Kethren was not my escort. He was my permission.”

“If they classify me, they will take me. They have a category for me. I do not want to be that category.”

“Above I was a person who did things for money. Here I am a thing that did things and now has to be filed.”

What he knows

  • He was retrieved after his Phase 6 contingency-escape. He does not know who specifically retrieved him; his memory is partial.
  • He was held in a place that may have been this waystation or one like it, for an unknown period (his sense of time is bad).
  • The route considered him valuable enough to recover but unreliable enough to flag.
  • He does not know who “E.” is. He has seen the mark on dispatches that came through his surface offices but never asked.

Mechanical use

Haskur can be offered as a “recovered asset” at the Black Ledger Gate (Method E: False Category, “recovered asset and escort”). This works because his classification is real. The party gives the route what it wants and passes through.

The party should feel uncomfortable doing this. Offering Haskur to the gate is offering a living person as cargo, even if Haskur consents to ease passage.

State B — Escaped or moved on

Haskur is not with the party. He may have been moved on through the Black Ledger Gate (the recovery route) by the route's own personnel before the party arrived. The party can find evidence he was here recently:

  • The H.V.A. line on the Witness Registry Wall.
  • Andren slips in the Burn Furnace basket.
  • Boot polish trace on the eighth landing.

The party will not encounter him in Phase 9. They may encounter him in Phase 10 if they follow the recovery route.

State C — Dead

Haskur is dead (killed by route forces, killed by the party in a prior phase, died of injury after Phase 6, etc.). The route has filed his transfer line as “failed recovery; substituted witness.” The Witness Registry Wall reflects this; the Recovery Crate Tag does not name him directly but the “substituted witness” note is on a related entry.

The conspiracy continues without him. This is the campaign's point about Haskur: he is replaceable. The party may encounter the substitute witness in Phase 10.

Statblock suggestion (State A only)

Use Haskur's Phase 1/Phase 6 statblock at diminished capacity:

  • Mage analog, CR 4 (down from Phase 6's CR 5/6 if used as antagonist).
  • HP 50 (down from full). AC 13.
  • Loss of one spell slot per level — he is recovering from his Phase 6 escape.
  • Significant Wisdom and Charisma damage from his recovery process — disadvantage on Persuasion checks; vulnerable to Insight reads.
  • Knows fewer spells than he did in Phase 6 — specifically, his most aggressive options are unavailable. He has been altered.

Kethren Ilvath — Conditional Use

Returning NPC. Significantly more important in Phase 9 than in prior phases. Use in one of three states.

State A — Alive and with the party

Kethren is the most useful and the most dangerous companion the party can have in Phase 9. He knows the waystation, in a way no surface PC can. He will read marks the party cannot. He will refuse questions the party most wants answered.

Behavior

  • He moves through the chambers like someone returning to a place they know well. He does not look at the walls; he glances.
  • He refuses broad questions. Any question that asks “what is this place,” “who runs this,” “what is your role” is deflected.
  • He answers narrow questions: “Is this the route?” (Yes.) “Do they expect us?” (Yes and no.) “Is the warden here?” (Yes; he is at the post.)
  • He fears recall. If route personnel address him by his courier mark, he tenses; if any of them say “recall,” he visibly recoils. This is the most important physical signal he gives. Recall, for him, is worse than death.
  • He may attempt to escape into a side passage at the first opportunity — particularly the Cistern Slit, which he knows well.

Useful lines

“You are still thinking in roads.”

“The gate does not open for names.”

“Below this, mercy is a kind of poor recordkeeping.”

“I will tell you what I am willing to tell you. I am not willing to tell you why.”

“You are good. I have not been around people who were good in some time.”

“Do not let them recall me. I will open the gate for you. Do not let them recall me.”

What he knows

  • How to read most of the marks at the waystation, including the deeper-authority mark (which he will not explain, and will not translate even under pressure).
  • Route procedure for the Black Ledger Gate.
  • The next several waystations on the escort route (he uses it routinely).
  • Haskur's status as “recoverable but unreliable.”
  • That his own authority extends two waystations further; beyond that, he is also subject to route classification.

What he does not know — or will not say

  • His own origin. He will not say where he is from. He will not confirm species.
  • What “recall” means in practical terms beyond “moving back down the route to a place from which return is not procedurally available.” He will not describe the place. He does not want to think about it.
  • “E.’s” identity. He knows the mark. He may know the figure. He will not say.
  • Why Blooming Rot was tested. He will say “I do not know,” and Insight DC 17 confirms he is telling the truth — he genuinely does not know the operation's deeper purpose.

Mechanical use

Kethren is the cleanest pathway through the Black Ledger Gate (Method B: Escort Authority). The cost is leverage; he expects something for using his authority.

If captured uncooperative: he will not open the gate. He will not betray the route. He will, eventually, accept bargains that include his own physical removal from the route's reach (Persuasion DC 18).

State B — Escaped or ahead of the party

Kethren's mark appears fresh throughout the waystation. He passed through in the last week. The party may glimpse him in the distance (a figure entering the Marker Gallery as the party leaves the Account Hall, a flicker at the Black Ledger Gate as it opens for him).

Do not force him into combat in Phase 9 if he is in this state. He should be a presence the party feels but cannot catch. Phase 10 may give them another chance.

State C — Dead

Kethren is dead. His route token, his fingerstamp, his mark on surface evidence — all of it can still serve as evidence. His death may have triggered route irregularity; the waystation's records show a recent “substituted authority” entry, indicating the route is having to find a new courier to fill his role.

The route does not grieve. It administers.

Statblock suggestion (State A only)

Mobile courier with evasive movement and precision strikes:

  • CR 4. HP 65. AC 16.
  • Speed 40 ft. Cunning Action (Dash/Disengage/Hide as bonus action).
  • Multiattack: 2 dagger attacks per round, each at +7 to hit, 1d4+5 piercing.
  • Route-Mask. While the mask is worn, ignores difficult terrain caused by smoke, dust, or low light; darkvision 60 ft.
  • Evasive. When subjected to an effect that allows a Dexterity save for half damage, takes no damage on success and half on failure.
  • Skills: Stealth +9, Insight +6, History +5, Investigation +5.
  • Equipment: route-mask, two daggers, courier satchel (with his bone fingerstamp), small leather case (the “second page” sealed dispatch).

V. Korre — Conditional Use

Returning NPC. By Phase 9, Korre's surface authority is severely diminished — the route has formally closed her channel. Use in one of three states.

State A — Alive and with the party

Korre is out of her jurisdiction. She knows it. She is afraid in a controlled way that her Greyhawk-handler persona usually conceals. Phase 9 is the first time the party sees her unsteady.

Behavior

  • She reads the Account Hall's tally marks with visible concentration. She has seen route shorthand only in summary — never on a wall, never at scale. This is bigger than she imagined.
  • Her surface papers do not work below. The Korre channel closure has propagated; her credentials are formally void.
  • She knows route language well enough to translate the registry, the conversion note, and (with effort) the warden's instructions. She did not know the route had this many categories.
  • She will not attempt to escape; she has nowhere to go. She is a surface handler whose surface authority just ended.

Useful lines

“My office ended above the first tally wall.”

“I have never been below it. I was not authorised below it.”

“I converted surface names. I did not know how thoroughly they were converted.”

“If I had known how many categories there were, I would have asked for one of my own.”

“I am not Kethren's superior. I do not think I ever was. I think I was told I was.”

What she knows

  • Surface-to-route conversion procedure. This is her specialty. She can translate any conversion note the party finds.
  • The names of every surface handler in Greyhawk City who fed assets into the route. This is the party's Phase 10 surface-investigation gold mine.
  • How “Andren” was constructed as Haskur's surface alias for processing. She did this paperwork.
  • That her own channel was closed by direct instruction from a route authority she has never met.

What she does not know

  • Anything below the conversion layer. She has never been on a route. She has never seen a tally wall before this.
  • “E.”'s identity.
  • What recall is.
  • Whether her own surface name is in the Burn Furnace queue.

Mechanical use

Korre is most useful for interpretation. She can translate marks the party would otherwise need expensive checks for. She gives the party her cooperation in exchange for one promise: get her back to the surface alive. She is not interested in justice. She is interested in survival.

State B — Escaped or not present

Korre is on the run on the surface, having survived Phase 7 or Phase 8 captivity. Phase 9 does not need her present. Her papers are still in the Old Route Office; her channel is still closed; her name may appear on a Burn Furnace surface-name slip alongside Andren's.

If the party returns to surface, Korre may be findable in Phase 10 — she will try to make contact with them, having realised that they are her only chance of negotiating a way out.

State C — Dead

Korre is dead. Her papers remain. Her channel remains closed. The route has filed her surface-side function as “closed; substitute not yet assigned.” The conspiracy needs to find a new handler. This is a Phase 10 vulnerability the party can exploit.

Statblock suggestion (State A only)

Use Korre's Phase 6 statblock unchanged. She is not fighting; she is reading and translating. Her surface-side combat capability is largely irrelevant in Phase 9.

Silent Courier — Generic Statblock

A subordinate route courier. Lower authority than Kethren; uses similar techniques and equipment. Not a major character; a drop-in for transit encounters. Distinct from the Phase 7 Silent Scout (which is a Cairn Hills surface-side scout).

Quick read

  • Indeterminate age, hard to tell. Lean. Always at full attention.
  • Grey-and-bone clothing, similar to Kethren but with simpler braid and no fingerstamp.
  • Smoked-quartz route-mask hanging at the belt. Reinforced boots. A small writing case with brass clasps at the hip.
  • Carries packets, dispatches, or sealed tokens for transfer.

Motive

Get past Tarnsmere and on to wherever they are going. The courier does not work for Korre directly. They work for the route's dispatch system. They will engage the party only if directly threatened, and even then they will prefer to disengage rather than die.

Public behavior

Will not speak unless forced. Will not stop unless cornered. Will use the cistern slit, side ducts, or any narrow space to evade. Will fight only to clear an escape route, then run.

Tell

The courier reaches for the route-mask at their belt when they are about to flee — checking it is still there before bolting.

Leverage

  • Capture alive (knock unconscious with non-lethal damage). A captured courier with a packet in their case is a major intelligence prize.
  • The courier is afraid of recall — the same fear Kethren has. Threats of being permanently held by the party may produce cooperation.
  • If the courier is carrying a packet for the deeper authority, taking the packet without taking the courier still produces useful intelligence.

What they know

  • The next two waystations on their route.
  • How to use route-masks, route signals, and route shorthand at basic proficiency.
  • One sealed dispatch's contents, if they have it open (they usually don't — couriers carry sealed packets blind).

What they do not know

  • Anything strategic. Couriers are functionaries.
  • “E.”'s identity.
  • The contents of sealed packets they carry.

Useful lines

(usually silent)

“Channel.”

“Authorised passage.”

“Escort declared.”

(if cornered) “I do not know what is in the packet. I am not allowed to know.”

(if interrogated heavily) “If you do not let me go, the route will recall me. I will tell you anything you want if you do not let them recall me.”

Statblock suggestion

Use Scout Captain (CR 3) base, with adjustments:

  • HP 58. AC 14.
  • Multiattack: short-sword + hand crossbow.
  • Speed 35 ft. Disengage as bonus action.
  • Evasive. When subjected to an effect that allows a Dexterity save for half damage, takes no damage on success and half on failure.
  • Route-Mask. While the mask is worn, ignores difficult terrain caused by smoke, dust, or low light; darkvision 60 ft.
  • Skills: Stealth +7, Survival +5, Perception +6.
  • Equipment: short-sword, hand crossbow with 20 bolts, route-mask, writing case (with route notes from past three crossings + one sealed dispatch).

The sealed dispatch is the key prize. Reading it (Investigation DC 14, History DC 16 for full translation) tells the party where the courier was going and roughly what category of business they were carrying.

Tally Sentinel — Optional Construct

A route construct that wakes only if the Permission Door above was forced in Phase 8, or if the Black Ledger Gate is forced in Phase 9, or if the Alert Clock reaches 5. Standing in a wall niche in the gate antechamber, currently dormant.

Description

Roughly humanoid, four feet tall. Body assembled from waystation mason-work blocks — the same dressed stone with the same black mineral seams. Stone tablet for a face, bearing a single incised mark — the deeper-authority mark (the shallow arc with three notches). Mute. Does not speak. Does not attack first. Its function is to witness procedural irregularities.

What it does

  • When awakened (forced gate, Alert 5), the sentinel steps from its niche and takes a position in the corridor adjacent to the irregularity.
  • It does not attack unless attacked. It observes.
  • If a PC drops to 0 HP within 5 feet, the sentinel uses its reaction to witness: it presses its tablet-face to the unconscious PC's forehead. The PC is not damaged. The sentinel's tablet faintly warms. This is the construct fulfilling its function. A PC who is witnessed in this way is marked — invisible to mundane senses but detectable by Detect Magic at the Final Threshold Landing and beyond. The descent below now knows that PC's name in the way the door knows it — as a record. This may matter in Phase 10 and beyond.
  • If the party leaves the gate area, the sentinel follows at a slow walking pace. It will not enter the Lower Route Fork chamber; it stops at the threshold.
  • If the party engages it in combat, it fights. It does not pursue past the antechamber.

Carry-forward

If the sentinel witnessed any PC at 0 HP, that PC carries a route-mark. In Phase 10 and beyond, this mark may cause:

  • Route personnel to recognise the PC immediately as “witnessed previously.”
  • Route gates to respond differently to them — either more permissively (they have been recorded) or more obstructively (they have been recorded as irregular).
  • Detect Magic to reveal the mark to allied surface clerics or arcanists.

Statblock

Construct, CR 4:

  • HP 52 (7d10 + 14). AC 17.
  • Speed 25 ft.
  • STR 16 (+3), DEX 9 (-1), CON 16 (+3), INT 1 (-5), WIS 3 (-4), CHA 1 (-5).
  • Multiattack: 3 stone-fist attacks per turn. Each at +6 to hit, 1d8 + 4 bludgeoning.
  • Damage Immunities: poison, psychic.
  • Damage Resistances: bludgeoning, piercing, and slashing from nonmagical attacks not made with adamantine weapons.
  • Condition Immunities: blinded, charmed, deafened, exhaustion, frightened, paralyzed, petrified, poisoned.
  • Senses: blindsight 60 ft. (can't see beyond it), passive Perception 6.
  • Languages: none.

Special: Witnessing (reaction, 1/encounter)

When a creature within 5 feet drops to 0 HP, the sentinel may use its reaction to press its tablet to the creature's forehead. The creature takes no damage. The creature is marked; the mark persists indefinitely and is detectable by Detect Magic.

If defeated

The construct falls apart into its component stones. The mason work matches the waystation exactly — the body was assembled from the waystation's own structural reserves. The tablet face, intact, can be carried away. The deeper-authority mark on the tablet, made portable, may be valuable to surface researchers.

If not engaged

The sentinel can be ignored. It will follow the party at walking pace, observing, and stop at the Lower Route Fork. It does not pursue past the threshold. The party can simply walk past it.

What this construct teaches

The route does not need active personnel to defend itself. It has built defenders into the architecture. The party should understand: every chamber of the waystation has the potential to wake an observer. They are being recorded whenever they choose to be.
